steps.description: Choose the right form of cos 2x
steps.explanation: The equation already contains a $\sin x$ term, so the aim is to write everything in terms of $\sin x$ alone. Of the three versions of the cosine double-angle formula, $\cos 2x = 1 - 2\sin^2 x$ is the one built from sines, so it will produce a single-variable equation.
Replacing $\cos 2x$ by $1 - 2\sin^2 x$ turns the mixed equation into one that only involves $\sin x$, which is exactly what we need to make progress.
Multiplying out the bracket removes the grouping so that we can gather all the terms together in the next step.
Bringing the $3$ across to the left makes the equation equal to zero, the standard set-up for a quadratic before we solve it.
Multiplying every term by $-1$ gives a positive $\sin^2 x$ coefficient, which makes the quadratic easier to recognise and factor.
The expression is a perfect square because $4\sin^2 x - 4\sin x + 1$ matches $(2\sin x - 1)^2$. A repeated factor tells us there is really only one value of $\sin x$ to find.
Setting the repeated bracket to zero gives the single value $\sin x = \tfrac{1}{2}$.
Since $\sin x$ can only take values between $-1$ and $1$, we confirm $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is possible before finding the angles.
The inverse sine gives the first-quadrant angle whose sine is $\tfrac{1}{2}$, which is $30^\circ$.
Sine is also positive in the second quadrant, and the symmetry rule $\sin(180^\circ - x) = \sin x$ gives the partner angle $150^\circ$.
In the third and fourth quadrants sine is negative, so $\sin x = \tfrac12$ has no solutions there. These are the only two angles in the interval.
Substituting $x = 30^\circ$ back into the original equation gives $3$, so this solution is genuine.
Here $2x = 300^\circ$ and $\cos 300^\circ = \tfrac12$, while $\sin 150^\circ = \tfrac12$, so the left side is again $3$. Both solutions check out.
These are the only values of $x$ in $0 \le x < 360^\circ$ that satisfy the equation.
